topic: A person learns their childhood bully became a therapist
raw_output:
[story 1]

The scent of lemon polish in Dr. Albright’s office always made my jaw clench. I stood by the door, knuckles white where I gripped my worn messenger bag, the silence of the room amplifying the thrumming beneath my ribs. He was sitting behind the mahogany desk, his posture impeccable, a gentle, knowing smile playing on his lips. It was Mark. The boy who used to dismantle my lunch money and leave bruises on my arm. Now, he wore a tweed jacket and listened to my anxieties as if they were delicate porcelain. "So," he began, his voice a low, measured balm, "tell me about this persistent feeling of being undervalued." I shifted my weight, the leather of my bag creaking under the sudden tension. My breath hitched, a shallow, ragged thing. I wanted to lash out, to shatter the carefully constructed calm he exuded, but the words caught in my throat, thick and useless.

[story 2]

I watched him from across the room, my fists clenched so tight my nails dug crescent moons into my palms. Every soft word he offered, every empathetic nod, felt like a deliberate slight. He spoke of trauma and coping mechanisms with an effortless grace that felt entirely undeserved. *He* was the one who used to make my world shrink, to make every hallway a minefield. Now, he held the keys to my fractured self. When he leaned forward, his eyes—those same startling blue eyes—met mine, there was a flicker of something I couldn't decipher, a shadow of the old antagonism surfacing before he smoothed it over with practiced kindness. I took a sharp, involuntary step backward, the floorboards protesting beneath my sudden movement.

[story 3]

"It's just perspective, isn't it?" he murmured, tapping a pen against a notepad, his attention entirely focused on me. The sound was infuriatingly soothing. I felt a hot pressure building behind my eyes, a desperate need to push him away, to make him see the raw, jagged edges of the person he was trying to mend. I crossed my arms tightly over my chest, a physical barrier against the perceived intimacy of the session. My jaw tightened until a dull ache radiated up my neck. I wanted to scream that his gentle approach was a mockery, that he didn't understand the sharp, corrosive residue of his past actions. I just stared at the polished wood of the desk, refusing to meet his gaze, letting the sheer force of my internal resistance radiate outward.
